# Transcription (Document D1052)

**Source:** Personal letter / oral history transcription
**Author:** Lena Stevens, 33 B Walker Street, Cooktown
**Telephone:** 07 40 69 6524
**Subject:** Family history — founding of Mossman Gorge Aboriginal Mission Station

---

My grand-father was born in Cooktown on North Shore across from the wharf. His playground was the coloured sands; having to travel through swamp, mangroves and scrub to get there and meet up with all of their people.

My grand-father described his father as a well known person with a high up position and a real businessman. Years later he went with his new family to Mossman, as his step father owned a beche-de-mer smoking plant on North Shore the work took him out to sea as well as to his shops in Mossman.

On the way to Mossman the youngest child was placed on a rock while they rested. He was forgotten and got left there. The child was picked up by another group going the same way and returned to the family because they knew who he belonged to.

Later on the boys mother left to meet up with her tribe and was late getting back. The father got upset and put the young boys in the care of someone who helped them. Their mother returned but found it hard to settle down in a new place.

The boys were expected to work for their keep and when the authorities found out they placed them in the Yarrabah Aboriginal Prison. When as they got older their father collected them to come to work in his shops. My grand father who was the eldest chose to stay in the "boys dormitory" to learn things. The other three were later sent to the Palm Island Prison.

Half caste children became government property.

---

Eventually my grand father became a carpenter and a musician and he played with the Yarrabah band.

He married and the couple had a child at Yarrabah before moving back to Mossman to live. He found work with farmers there and with his family increasing he acquired a large area of land in a hand shake deal. He was told no one can take it from him or his family and would belong to his children and grand-children for generations to come.

The man built his own home and cleared land enough to grow what he needed.

When he heard that more of his people were getting out and had no where to go he established a base for them to call home. He didn't want any of his people to get sent back if they were found by the authorities (the police) as having no where to live or stay.

Thus began the Mossman Gorge Aboriginal Mission Station.

Because of his colour my grand-father could not be in charge or have any say over matters concerning such a settlement. He was also not allowed to associate with his own (aboriginal) people. After contacting the authorities on several occasions the government finally allowed three missionaries to be in charge of the place.

They were Miss Hale, Miss Heatherington who stayed for some length of time. The third person could not endure the living conditions and left due to illness & homesickness.

My grandfather also let a parcel of land to a group of Chinese people, families for some time. It was situated between the mission area and his own useable land. There were around thirty to fifty men, women and children living there. They were there for some time and were growing their own vegetables. As their disappearance from the place was gradual they may have returned to their own country. It is believed however that some who died could be buried there as as children we were not allowed to walk on certain areas.

Around 1928-29 an all night corroboree took place all along the front of my grand-parents house to welcome my grandmother to the land; the area of her birth. He said "they came all painted up, a big mob" to dance for me. The sun was coming up and they were still dancing – they were, as she said, "still kicking up dust". Even when they got tired they all started to slowly go away.

The land give to her was past the hill where that mill is (Murdochs house is there) down past Zamtoms barracks then across the river that way to the place called "Broken Nose".

Sincerely
Lena Stevens
